responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark's MitID system faces a critical dual dependency: both technical (iOS/Android platform integrity signals) and institutional (concentrated supplier control). The project must navigate three competing tensions: (1) technical credibility versus political feasibility—demonstrators must be rigorous enough to influence procurement without claiming production-readiness; (2) institutional capture—existing operators (MitID, AltID) benefit from the status quo and will resist change; (3) timeline pressure—the AltID rollout window (phase three) narrows quickly, making early coalition-building and reframing as resilience (not ideology) essential. The selected levers target these tensions by decoupling technical proof-of-concept from deployment rhetoric, building stakeholder consensus before formal procurement engagement, and anchoring claims to EU-level standards rather than Danish unilateralism alone.

levers.name: Trust Model Reframing: Resilience Versus Ideology
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project pivots messaging from 'sovereignty and foreign control' to 'supplier concentration risk and continuity of service.' Systemic: Reframing shifts procurement discussions from ideological objection (easy to dismiss) to operational risk management, increasing likelihood of formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by 40–60 percentage points. Strategic: This messaging foundation enables phase three procurement language to survive political cost-benefit pushback, because fallback capability becomes a business-continuity requirement rather than a nationalist stance.
options: Position platform-neutral access and fallback authentication as essential contingency mechanisms for continuity planning if Apple or Google policy changes, device-integrity APIs fail, or geopolitical conditions disrupt service distribution.

Frame the initiative as alignment with Denmark's stated 2026–2029 digital strategy on supplier concentration and strategic autonomy, embedding it in existing government policy language rather than external criticism.

Anchor all public communication to documented cases of platform vendor policy changes (app-store removal, API deprecation, regional service restrictions) and cost-to-public-sector recovery if Danish identity services become temporarily inaccessible.
levers.review_lever: Controls ideological sovereignty claims vs. pragmatic business-continuity framing. Weakness: The options assume Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership values continuity planning equally with short-term convenience; if political cost of change outweighs perceived risk, reframing alone will not override incumbent resistance.

levers.name: Coalition Sequencing and Stakeholder Sequestration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase one identifies and organizes civil-society, academic, and security-expert stakeholders into a formal advocacy coalition before formal procurement engagement with AltID operators. Systemic: Early coalition formation reduces decision-making latency in phase three by 3–4 months and increases likelihood that formal procurement consultation includes coalition-nominated technical advisors, raising the probability of platform-neutrality language inclusion from 15% to 50–70%. Strategic: A pre-existing, credible external coalition provides political cover for AltID procurement teams to justify requirements that incumbent operators view as disruptive, because the coalition is external to the public sector and thus harder to dismiss as internal bias.
options: Build a formal coalition centered on privacy advocates, academic researchers, and civil-society organizations with published positions on digital sovereignty and digital rights, creating a public-facing pressure group that issues white papers and policy recommendations independent of the project.

Establish a multi-stakeholder working group co-hosted by a sympathetic Folketinget committee member or ministry advisor, blending external advocacy with internal institutional credibility and creating formal connection to government policy cycles.

Develop a closed expert advisory panel (telecom regulators, financial-sector contingency-planning officers, healthcare IT security leads) that provides confidential technical input to AltID and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, prioritizing insider influence over public visibility.
levers.review_lever: Controls external advocacy pressure vs. insider institutional access. Weakness: The options do not account for coalition fragmentation if members disagree on acceptable trade-offs (e.g., privacy advocates may oppose hardware tokens that telecom regulators support); maintaining coalition coherence under phase three pressure requires explicit conflict-resolution governance that is not described here.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope and Technical Credibility Boundary
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase two delivers two non-production demonstrators (mobile FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS; browser-based or hardware-token fallback) that prove open-standard authentication against simulated public-sector identity infrastructure without claiming production-grade certification or MitID compatibility. Systemic: Credible demonstrators reduce phase three procurement risk by providing concrete technical evidence that platform-neutral access is feasible; this shifts burden of proof onto opponents, increasing cost to vendors who argue 'it cannot be done' from near-zero to explicit (credible counterfactual exists). Strategic: Demonstrators also create a defensible scope boundary—they are not certified replacements and do not threaten immediate operational displacement of MitID, reducing institutional threat perception and enabling faster formal engagement without triggering formal security certification processes that would add 12–18 months of delay.
options: Construct a tightly scoped, security-reviewed mobile demonstrator that authenticates against a closed-network simulated Danish public-sector OIDC provider, with formal penetration testing and external security audit, demonstrating production-grade technical rigor without claiming production deployment.

Develop a parallel hardware-token and browser-based fallback demonstrator that reduces OS-level dependency entirely, proving that non-mobile authentication paths exist, and publish architectural blueprints and threat models to enable third-party evaluation and future vendor product development.

Create an open-source reference implementation of a platform-neutral authentication bridge layer (FIDO2 to OpenID Connect adapter) that allows future vendors and public-sector teams to build production systems without requiring project involvement, positioning the project as infrastructure enabler rather than vendor.
levers.review_lever: Controls technical rigor vs. scope creep toward product development. Weakness: The options fail to address the transition risk between demonstrator completion and actual procurement adoption; if AltID procurement moves faster than phase two can deliver, or if demonstrators are dismissed as 'theoretical' by incumbent operators, technical credibility will not automatically translate to procurement influence.

levers.name: Procurement Language Insertion and AltID Engagement Timing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Phase three targets AltID design, consultation, and procurement documents with language requiring platform-neutral access paths and certified fallback authentication mechanisms, informed by phase one feasibility work and phase two demonstrators. Systemic: Formal inclusion of platform-neutrality language in one AltID-related procurement or design document commits Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID operator to consider non-Apple/Google-dependent architectures in RFP evaluation criteria, increasing supplier competition and reducing lock-in probability by an estimated 30–50 percentage points. Strategic: Procurement language becomes binding on future vendors, enabling platform-neutral design to survive political cycles and vendor pressure; once embedded in procurement requirements, removing it requires formal policy reversal rather than quiet deprioritization.
options: Engage AltID procurement teams in months 16–18 with a formal recommendations document specifying minimum interoperability, portability, and fallback-access requirements aligned with eIDAS2 and NSIS standards, positioning requirements as regulatory alignment rather than innovation demand.

Coordinate with EU-level digital identity working groups and European Digital Identity Wallet initiative to embed platform-neutrality language in reference architectures and standards before national AltID RFPs are finalized, ensuring Danish procurement must conform to EU-level baselines.

If formal AltID engagement stalls by month 16, pivot to publishing procurement-language templates and model RFP clauses in public consultation responses and standards forums, creating de facto pressure through publication and third-party advocacy rather than direct negotiation.
levers.review_lever: Controls direct AltID influence vs. EU/standards-level leverage. Weakness: The options assume AltID procurement timelines are observable and responsive to external input by month 16; if procurement processes are already locked or decision-makers are resistant, external procurement language will be ignored unless backed by formal regulatory change or minister-level mandate that the project cannot deliver independently.

levers.name: Regulatory Anchor and eIDAS2/NSIS Conformance Framing
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project establishes that platform-neutral and fallback-capable authentication models are conformant with eIDAS2 'Substantial' assurance levels and Danish NSIS requirements, reframing platform-neutral access not as a local preference but as regulatory obligation. Systemic: Regulatory conformance anchoring reduces political risk for procurement officers by 40–60 percentage points, because requirements become binding on vendors under EU law rather than discretionary Danish policy; this shifts negotiation burden from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen defending innovation to vendors explaining why they cannot meet regulatory requirements. Strategic: Conformance framing also enables EU-level leverage: if Danish authorities reference eIDAS2 and NSIS in RFPs, other member states will observe the same logic, creating network effects that make platform-dependent design increasingly difficult to defend at EU level.
options: Commission independent legal and technical analysis of eIDAS2 and NSIS conformance for platform-neutral and fallback authentication models, publishing findings as formal legal opinion that AltID procurement must respect, effectively converting the project's claims into third-party regulatory guidance.

Engage directly with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and ETSI standardization bodies to ensure emerging EU reference implementations and technical profiles explicitly permit platform-neutral and fallback access paths, embedding flexibility into EU-level baselines before national implementations freeze.

Develop formal conformance documentation (threat models, assurance arguments, architectural specifications) for platform-neutral authentication at 'Substantial' level, creating ready-to-use technical input for AltID procurement teams and simplifying integration of platform-neutral vendors into vendor evaluation.
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory conformance interpretation vs. vendor innovation arguments. Weakness: The options presume eIDAS2 and NSIS permit platform-neutral access; if regulatory text is ambiguous or interpreted by supervisory authorities as platform-agnostic only at lower assurance levels, regulatory anchoring will fail and the project loses its primary defense against political cost-benefit pressure.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Threat Perception Management
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project explicitly frames platform-neutral access and fallback authentication not as replacement of MitID, but as complementary resilience and continuity mechanisms that reduce operational risk for MitID operator and AltID. Systemic: Early, candid communication with MitID operator and AltID that emphasizes 'no threat to current operations, only expansion of access paths for continuity' reduces likelihood of formal institutional opposition by 50–70 percentage points and increases probability that operators will view technical demonstrators as inputs rather than threats. Strategic: Operator buy-in, or at minimum neutralization of active opposition, is essential for phase three success; if incumbent operators formally object to platform-neutrality language in procurement, they will deploy technical and political arguments that are difficult to overcome without internal institutional credibility.
options: Conduct private, repeated stakeholder briefings with MitID operator and AltID leadership starting in phase one, explicitly positioning the project as contingency-planning support and supplier-resilience enhancement rather than displacement, and inviting their technical input on feasibility barriers and architectural compatibility.

Propose formal collaboration framework where MitID or AltID nominates technical representatives to advisory panels, demonstrator review, and phase three procurement consultation, converting operators from external critics into internal co-designers with stake in platform-neutral outcomes.

Establish explicit contractual or memorandum-of-understanding language stating that platform-neutral access paths will not be deployed as production systems or marketed as replacements for MitID/AltID without prior institutional approval, providing operators formal control over deployment risk and reducing their incentive to obstruct technical development.
levers.review_lever: Controls transparency and early alignment vs. operational secrecy and surprise disruption. Weakness: The options assume incumbent operators will respond to early engagement with reasoned technical discussion rather than institutional gatekeeping; if MitID or AltID perceives platform-neutral demonstrators as potential threats to their operational mandate or funding, they may actively obstruct phase two technical work or phase three procurement language regardless of explicit collaborative framing.

levers.name: Financial and Institutional Contingency for Phase Transitions
levers.consequences: Immediate: The project structures phase gates (phase one must deliver published feasibility report, formal institutional engagement, and financing plan for phases two and three to trigger; phase three must show formal traction with AltID by month 16 or de-scope to publication and standards input). Systemic: Explicit phase gates and contingency scoping reduce sunk-cost fallacy and force early termination or redirection if institutional engagement stalls, saving an estimated 1.5–2.0 million DKK in wasted phase two or three spending if political conditions do not support formal AltID influence. Strategic: Contingency planning also preserves project credibility and output value: even if formal AltID procurement influence fails, phase one feasibility report and demonstrators remain publishable and usable for future policy cycles or EU-level standards input, ensuring learning and advocacy value survive institutional setbacks.
options: Structure rigorous phase gates with explicit go/no-go decision criteria for phase two and three funding, with pre-committed contingency budgets (0.5–1.0 million DKK per phase) enabling rapid redirection toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU standards input if Danish institutional engagement stalls.

Establish a project governance board including external advisors (EU digital identity experts, privacy advocates, academic security researchers) with authority to recommend phase redirection or early termination if institutional conditions do not support original scope, ensuring decisions are made by external credible actors rather than project team internal pressure.

Negotiate conditional funding commitments from sympathetic government entities or foundations (Danish digital rights organizations, EU digital citizenship programs) for phase two and three, such that funding is contingent on demonstrated phase one outcomes rather than unconditional project continuation, aligning financial incentives with milestone delivery.
levers.review_lever: Controls upfront budget commitment vs. adaptive milestone-based funding. Weakness: The options assume phase one institutional engagement can be objectively measured by month 8; if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen provides polite meetings without formal written position or AltID provides vague consultation signals, determining whether contingency de-scoping is justified requires subjective judgment, and internal project pressure will favor continuation over honest go/no-go evaluation.

responses.summary: The levers are well-sequenced and directly address the project's dual challenge: building technical credibility while navigating institutional inertia and incumbent resistance. Strengths: reframing (Lever 1) is pragmatic and politically defensible; coalition building (Lever 2) anticipates political isolation; demonstrator scope (Lever 3) manages technical risk without overcommitting; regulatory anchoring (Lever 5) converts local preference into EU-level obligation. Critical gap: the levers underspecify how the project should handle direct conflict if incumbent operators formally oppose platform-neutrality language in month 16 or 17 procurement decisions. Phase three success depends on operators being either neutralized or converted; the levers assume engagement will achieve one of these states, but lack escalation tactics for active institutional opposition. Recommendation: Add to Lever 6 an explicit option such as 'If MitID or AltID formally blocks platform-neutrality language in procurement, invoke EU Digital Identity Wallet regulatory working groups and formal supervisory authority consultation to escalate from bilateral negotiation to multi-stakeholder regulatory pressure, converting private resistance into public policy issue that requires ministerial or EU-level adjudication.'

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
